Investigates cellular immune responses in chronic human viral infection, with particular focus on HIV-specific CD8+ and CD4+ T-cell responses and mechanisms by which rare 'elite controllers' suppress HIV without antiretroviral therapy. Discoveries include the first descriptions of HIV-specific CD8+ and CD4+ T cells and the role of immune checkpoints in T-cell exhaustion during chronic infection. Founding Director of the Ragon Institute of MGH, MIT, and Harvard; HHMI Investigator; co-founder of the Africa Health Research Institute in KwaZulu-Natal.

Studies micro/nanoscale heat and mass transfer for thermal management, water harvesting, desalination, and solar thermal energy conversion. Notable work includes a metal-organic-framework (MOF)-based atmospheric water harvester that pulls drinkable water from desert air, a passive multistage solar still for high-efficiency desalination, and nanostructured surfaces for boiling/condensation enhancement and electronics cooling. Methods combine micro/nanofabrication, in-situ ESEM imaging, and continuum heat-transfer modeling.

Studies neural circuits governing bidirectional brain-body interactions — the sensory and affective dimensions of pain and addiction, central pain-suppression by general anesthesia, and sensorimotor circuits for active touch, eating, drinking, and movement. Methods include monosynaptic rabies-virus circuit tracing, AAV-based intersectional genetic targeting, two-photon and miniscope calcium imaging in behaving mice, optogenetic and chemogenetic manipulation, and fiber photometry. Co-directs the K. Lisa Yang and Hock E. Tan Center for Molecular Therapeutics.

Studies neural circuits governing bidirectional brain-body interactions — the sensory and affective dimensions of pain and addiction, central pain-suppression by general anesthesia, and sensorimotor circuits for active touch, eating, drinking, and movement. Methods include monosynaptic rabies-virus circuit tracing, AAV-based intersectional genetic targeting, two-photon and miniscope calcium imaging in behaving mice, optogenetic and chemogenetic manipulation, and fiber photometry. Co-directs the K. Lisa Yang and Hock E. Tan Center for Molecular Therapeutics.

Develops nanoengineered advanced composites and bulk nanostructured materials for aerospace structures and multifunctional applications — including aligned carbon nanotube (A-CNT) reinforced laminates, carbon-based material synthesis, and additively manufactured structures. Recent work on 'nanostitching' uses CNT arrays to toughen weak interlaminar regions in composites, producing lighter, more damage-tolerant aircraft skins. The group also builds power-MEMS devices (micro fuel cells, energy harvesters) and damage-sensing structural composites.

Uses the transparent, genetically tractable jellyfish Clytia hemisphaerica as a model organism to study nervous system evolution, development, regeneration, and function at the interface of systems and evolutionary neuroscience. Stable F1 transgenic Clytia lines support cell-type-specific ablation and whole-organism GCaMP calcium imaging of neuronal activity. Research asks how a simple, decentralized nervous system remains robust to continuous neuron turnover and large-scale injury.

The Wendlandt group develops new selective catalytic reactions for organic synthesis, especially methods that can isomerize alkenes or epimerize sugars site-selectively to access otherwise hard-to-make stereoisomers. The hallmark approach uses dual catalysis — pairing a photoredox catalyst with a hydrogen-atom-transfer or transition-metal catalyst — to break and re-form bonds at remote positions on a molecule. Work spans organometallic synthesis, physical organic chemistry, mechanism studies, and total synthesis of carbohydrates and natural products.

l: Whittle Research Group (Geotechnical Engineering & Underground Infrastructure)
s: Whittle's group works on geotechnical engineering — predicting how soils and underground structures deform and fail. The lab develops constitutive models of clays and sands within elasto-plasticity, simulates coupled thermo-hydro-mechanical soil behavior, and applies these models to deepwater oil-platform foundations and major urban tunneling projects (Boston's Big Dig, Tren Urbano in San Juan). Methods are continuum mechanics, finite-element modeling, large-scale lab and field experiments on soils, and instrumentation of real construction sites.

The Willard Group is a theoretical/computational physical chemistry lab studying how nanoscale disorder controls emergent behavior in liquids, biological interfaces, and energy materials. Active topics include electron dynamics in conjugated/organic semiconductors, water structure around proteins and electrodes, and electrochemistry at disordered interfaces. The group's methods are molecular dynamics simulation, statistical mechanics, coarse-grained modeling, and machine-learning analysis of simulation trajectories.

Macroeconomist and time-series econometrician developing new identification strategies for the causes of business cycles and for the effects of monetary and fiscal policy shocks. Work covers the 'missing intercept' problem in cross-sectional-to-aggregate demand translation, heterogeneous-agent macro models, and the econometrics of impulse-response estimation (local projections vs. VARs). Faculty Research Fellow at the NBER and 2024 Kiel Institute Excellence Award recipient.

Investigates solid-state ionic-electronic materials and their surfaces/interfaces for next-generation energy conversion and information-processing devices, including solid oxide fuel cells, water and CO2 electrolysis, solid-state Li-ion batteries, memristive and brain-inspired analog computing, and corrosion-resistant films. Focuses on how perovskite-oxide cathodes and anodes evolve at the surface under electrochemical, thermal, and gas stimuli to elucidate degradation mechanisms. Methods include in situ X-ray spectroscopy, scanning probe microscopy, and first-principles (DFT) modeling.

Engineering researcher working on the mathematical co-design of complex autonomous and mobility systems, formalizing the joint optimization of hardware, software, and infrastructure choices for self-driving vehicles, robots, and urban transportation networks. Uses applied category theory (compositionality of design problems), monotone optimization on partial orders, game theory, and reinforcement learning to make system-level trade-offs computationally tractable. Applications include AV fleet sizing, sensor selection, and policy design.

Develops and applies CRISPR-based tools for genome and transcriptome engineering, having engineered SpCas9 for mammalian genome editing and discovered/characterized Cas12, Cas13, and other class 2 CRISPR effectors via computational mining of microbial genomes. Built the SHERLOCK nucleic-acid diagnostic platform (Cas13-based) and REPAIR/RESCUE RNA base editors, plus AAV-deliverable compact CRISPR systems for in vivo therapy. Methods include structural biology of Cas effectors, directed evolution, deep mutational scanning, and large-scale CRISPR screens in primary cells and organoids.

Develops and applies CRISPR-based tools for genome and transcriptome engineering, having engineered SpCas9 for mammalian genome editing and discovered/characterized Cas12, Cas13, and other class 2 CRISPR effectors via computational mining of microbial genomes. Built the SHERLOCK nucleic-acid diagnostic platform (Cas13-based) and REPAIR/RESCUE RNA base editors, plus AAV-deliverable compact CRISPR systems for in vivo therapy. Methods include structural biology of Cas effectors, directed evolution, deep mutational scanning, and large-scale CRISPR screens in primary cells and organoids.

Theoretical physicist working in string theory, with central contributions to string field theory — the early construction of the field theory of open strings and subsequent development of closed string field theory. Additional work covers D-branes with exceptional symmetry, tachyon condensation, and the AdS/CFT correspondence. Author of the undergraduate textbook A First Course in String Theory (Cambridge).
